content.text: Most of the world's information is behind a web interface: search results, forms, dashboards, product pages, and single-page applications. An agent that can only call APIs covers a fraction of that surface. A
can navigate to any URL, read the page, fill in forms, click buttons, and extract structured data — the same operations a human user performs.
The standard library for programmatic browser control in Python is

. It controls Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit headlessly (no screen required). Playwright actions are the natural tools to expose to an LLM agent: navigate to a URL, extract text, take a screenshot, click an element, fill a form field.
The page's title and text content
Visible text for the model to read
PNG bytes — pass to a vision model
Install Playwright and its Chromium binary before writing agent tools. Playwright provides both a synchronous API (
). The sync API is simpler for scripts and tools; the async API fits web frameworks.

pip install playwright
playwright install chromium

from playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright

def fetch_page_text(url: str) -> str:
    """Navigate to a URL and return the page's visible text content."""
    with sync_playwright() as pw:
        browser = pw.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        page = browser.new_page()
        page.goto(url, wait_until="domcontentloaded", timeout=15_000)
        text = page.inner_text("body")
        browser.close()
    return text[:4000]  # truncate for the LLM context window

def take_screenshot(url: str, output_path: str) -> str:
    """Navigate to a URL and save a screenshot to output_path."""
    with sync_playwright() as pw:
        browser = pw.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        page = browser.new_page(viewport={"width": 1280, "height": 800})
        page.goto(url, wait_until="networkidle", timeout=20_000)
        page.screenshot(path=output_path, full_page=False)
        browser.close()
    return f"Screenshot saved to {output_path}"

# Example
text = fetch_page_text("https://example.com")
print(text[:200])
# Example Domain
# This domain is for use in illustrative examples in documents.
# You may use this domain in literature without prior coordination or asking for permission.
context manager starts the Playwright process.
parameter controls when the call returns:
returns when the HTML is parsed (fast),
waits until there are no more network requests (reliable for SPAs but slower). Always call
— Playwright browsers are OS-level processes that outlive the script if not closed.
Building Browser Tools for an Agent

Wrap Playwright operations as typed functions that an LLM can call. Each tool should do one thing, return text the model can read, and handle errors gracefully. Reuse a single browser instance across tool calls to avoid the overhead of launching a new Chromium process for every action.

from playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright, Browser, Page
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class BrowserSession:
    """A persistent browser session reused across agent tool calls."""
    headless: bool = True
    _browser: Optional[Browser] = field(default=None, repr=False)
    _page: Optional[Page] = field(default=None, repr=False)
    _pw: object = field(default=None, repr=False)

    def start(self) -> None:
        self._pw = sync_playwright().start()
        self._browser = self._pw.chromium.launch(headless=self.headless)
        self._page = self._browser.new_page(viewport={"width": 1280, "height": 800})

    def stop(self) -> None:
        if self._browser:
            self._browser.close()
            self._pw.stop()

    # --- Tools the agent can call ---

    def navigate(self, url: str) -> str:
        """Navigate to a URL. Returns the page title and first 2000 chars of body text."""
        try:
            self._page.goto(url, wait_until="domcontentloaded", timeout=15_000)
            title = self._page.title()
            text = self._page.inner_text("body")[:2000]
            return f"Title: {title}\n\nContent:\n{text}"
        except Exception as e:
            return f"Navigation error: {e}"

    def click(self, selector: str) -> str:
        """Click an element identified by a CSS selector or text."""
        try:
            self._page.click(selector, timeout=5_000)
            title = self._page.title()
            return f"Clicked '{selector}'. Now on page: {title}"
        except Exception as e:
            return f"Click error: {e}"

    def fill(self, selector: str, value: str) -> str:
        """Fill a form field (input/textarea) with a value."""
        try:
            self._page.fill(selector, value, timeout=5_000)
            return f"Filled '{selector}' with value."
        except Exception as e:
            return f"Fill error: {e}"

    def get_links(self) -> str:
        """Return all hyperlinks on the current page."""
        links = self._page.eval_on_selector_all(
            "a[href]",
            "els => els.map(e => ({text: e.innerText.trim(), href: e.href}))",
        )
        lines = [f"- {l['text'][:60]}: {l['href']}" for l in links[:20]]
        return "\n".join(lines) if lines else "No links found."

# Usage
session = BrowserSession(headless=True)
session.start()
try:
    print(session.navigate("https://example.com"))
    print(session.get_links())
finally:
    session.stop()
class holds a persistent browser and page across calls. Starting a new Chromium process per tool call adds ~500ms of overhead. By reusing the session, tool calls take 50–200ms. The
tool returns the page title and a text excerpt: this is what the model reads to decide its next action. The
tools use CSS selectors, which the model learns to produce from the page content it receives. Wrap the session in a
to ensure the browser is always closed.
With browser tools defined, wire them to an LLM agent. The agent reads the page, decides the next action, and calls the appropriate tool. This is a
: Reason about what the page shows, Act by calling a browser tool, observe the result, repeat.
The agent loop sends the current conversation to the model, executes any tool calls using the

messages, and continues until the model stops. The model navigates pages, reads their content, and decides what to do next — the same process a human follows when browsing the web. For more complex tasks (filling forms, handling login pages, navigating JavaScript-heavy apps), add
tools as the agent's needs grow.
For web applications (FastAPI, aiohttp), use Playwright's async API.
throughout — the browser operations yield to the event loop while waiting for the network. This lets many agent requests run concurrently in a single server process.

import asyncio
from playwright.async_api import async_playwright

async def fetch_page_text_async(url: str) -> str:
    """Async version: navigate to a URL and return visible text."""
    async with async_playwright() as pw:
        browser = await pw.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        page = await browser.new_page()
        await page.goto(url, wait_until="domcontentloaded", timeout=15_000)
        text = await page.inner_text("body")
        await browser.close()
    return text[:4000]

async def scrape_multiple(urls: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """Scrape multiple pages concurrently."""
    async with async_playwright() as pw:
        browser = await pw.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        async def scrape(url: str) -> str:
            page = await browser.new_page()
            try:
                await page.goto(url, timeout=10_000)
                text = await page.inner_text("body")
                return text[:2000]
            except Exception as e:
                return f"Error: {e}"
            finally:
                await page.close()

        results = await asyncio.gather(*[scrape(url) for url in urls])
        await browser.close()
    return list(results)

# Each page runs concurrently — total time ~ slowest single page
results = asyncio.run(scrape_multiple([
    "https://example.com",
    "https://example.org",
]))
for text in results:
    print(text[:100])
The async API follows the same structure as the sync API — add

runs multiple page scrapes concurrently: the total time is roughly the slowest single page, not the sum. For a web service that handles many simultaneous browser-agent requests, create one shared browser per server process and open a new page per request — pages are lightweight compared to browsers.
Playwright is the standard tool for giving agents browser access.

Wrap page operations (navigate, click, fill, get_links) as typed agent tools. Reuse a single BrowserSession across tool calls to avoid per-call launch overhead. Use the sync API for scripts and the async API for concurrent web-service agents. The agent loop reads page text, decides the next browser action, and continues until the task is complete — the same reasoning process a human uses when browsing.
